significance: Natural Law: Ethics grounded in cosmic order rather than arbitrary divine command
Social Contract: Individual ethics connected to cosmic maintenance
Merit-Based Afterlife: Judgment based on moral worth, not wealth or status
Universal Principles: Truth and justice as absolute standards applying to all
Cosmic Interdependence: Human actions affect divine realm and vice versa
name: Egyptian Mythology
definition: Ancient Egyptian concept of cosmic order, truth, justice, and balance - the fundamental principle maintaining the universe's integrity and governing both divine and human conduct.
examples: Judgment of the dead against the feather of Ma'at in the afterlife
Pharaoh's duty to maintain Ma'at through proper rituals and governance
Proper conduct in commerce, law, and daily relationships
Seasonal flooding of the Nile as expression of cosmic order
applications: Ethical codes for conduct and virtue

keyMyths.title: The Creation Myth: Atum and the Primordial Waters
keyMyths.description: From the chaotic, watery abyss of Nun, the self-created god Atum arose. He initiated creation through acts of will and, in some versions, through bodily functions, bringing forth the first gods and the cosmos from the void.

keyMyths.title: The Myth of Osiris and Isis
keyMyths.description: Osiris, the benevolent king, is murdered by his jealous brother Set. Isis, his devoted wife, searches for his body, resurrects him temporarily to conceive Horus, and ultimately restores him to life as the ruler of the underworld, becoming a symbol of resurrection and eternal life.

keyMyths.title: The Contendings of Horus and Set
keyMyths.description: After Osiris's death, Horus, his son, battles Set for the throne of Egypt in a series of trials and conflicts. The struggle represents the cosmic battle between order and chaos, ultimately resolved with Horus's victory and ascension as the rightful king.

keyMyths.title: Ra's Journey Through the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Each night, the sun god Ra journeys through the dangerous underworld (Duat), facing monstrous creatures and obstacles. He is reborn each morning, symbolizing the daily cycle of death and rebirth, and the triumph of light over darkness.

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart Ceremony
keyMyths.description: In the afterlife, the deceased's heart is weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). If the heart is lighter, the individual is deemed worthy and granted eternal life; if heavier, it is devoured by the monstrous Ammit, resulting in eternal oblivion.

sources.name: The Pyramid Texts
sources.description: Old Kingdom royal funerary texts inscribed on the walls of pyramids, providing insights into early Egyptian beliefs about the afterlife and royal divinity.
sources.title: The Pyramid Texts

sources.name: The Coffin Texts
sources.description: Middle Kingdom funerary texts inscribed on coffins, expanding access to afterlife beliefs beyond the royal family.
sources.title: The Coffin Texts

sources.name: The Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells and rituals intended to guide the deceased through the underworld and ensure a successful afterlife, dating from the New Kingdom onwards.
sources.title: The Book of the Dead

sources.name: Plutarch, *De Iside et Osiride*
sources.description: A Greek philosophical treatise providing a detailed account of the myth of Osiris and Isis, although interpreted through a Hellenistic lens.
sources.title: Plutarch, *De Iside et Osiride*

sources.name: Chester Beatty Papyrus I
sources.description: An important New Kingdom papyrus containing literary texts, including the Contendings of Horus and Set, providing insights into the myths and narratives of the time.
sources.title: Chester Beatty Papyrus I
